messages.text: By the eighth day my water supply was gone. The engine still refused to live, and thirst made every thought sharp.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Your stories are beautiful, but they do not give us water. We must search or die.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Water can also be good for the heart. My fox would understand.
We walked under the stars. My body wanted sleep, but his quiet faith gave comfort where reason gave none.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: The desert is beautiful because it hides a well somewhere. The essential thing may be invisible.
I carried him when he grew tired. He was light, almost like a flame, and the effort made my arms ache.
Near dawn we found a well. It stood in the sand like a village well, with rope, pulley, and bucket, though no village was near.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: This is impossible. Wells in the desert do not appear ready for travelers.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Everything is ready when a friend is thirsty.
I raised the bucket. The rope sang against the pulley, and the water shone in the morning light like a reward.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: I am thirsty for this water. Let me drink.
He drank with closed eyes. The water was not only water. It held the walk, the stars, the effort, and our shared silence.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: It is sweet. It tastes like a festival.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Because it was born from the search. What makes things precious is invisible work.
I remembered the fox, the rose, and even my first drawing. A memory can become a well if someone returns to it with love.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: You must keep your promise about the muzzle. Your sheep must not eat my rose.
The promise struck me with fear. I had drawn the sheep and the box, but not the strap for the muzzle.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: I will draw it today. You speak as if you are going far away.
He smiled with such comfort and sadness that I understood the reward of finding water had not ended our danger.
Something else waited in the desert, and his memory of home had become stronger than thirst.
The well sounded before it appeared. Its pulley creaked like music from a village festival, and that music made the water feel prepared for us.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: Men on trains rush after gardens and shops, yet they do not know what they search for.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: Only children press their faces to windows. They still believe the reward may be hidden nearby.
His answer comforted me because it did not deny thirst. It placed thirst inside a larger hunger for meaning, friendship, and memory.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: A single flower can make a whole desert precious if you believe it is somewhere under the stars.
I drank again and understood that the effort of drawing water had changed the water. What we carry together tastes different.
Then he reminded me of the muzzle, and the morning joy tilted toward fear. Even precious promises need practical details.
I had crossed the desert as a pilot seeking water; I returned from the well as a man who understood why children love hidden things.

messages.name: Little Prince
messages.text: The supply of water matters, but the memory of finding it together matters too.
His face grew serious again when he spoke of the rose, and the well seemed to echo the promise I had not completed.
When I lifted the bucket, I felt the rope pull through my hands like proof that invisible things still ask for physical effort.

messages.name: Narrator
messages.text: The water was clear, but what I tasted most was the walk that led us there.
The prince smiled because that was exactly what he had hoped I would understand.
